content.text: AWS Outposts brings AWS compute, storage, networking, and database services to your own data center or colocation facility. AWS ships a rack of AWS-managed hardware to your site; you get the same EC2 APIs, IAM, and SDK calls you use in any region — but the workload runs physically on-premises.

attrs.alt: AWS Outposts architecture: on-premises Outpost rack connected to parent AWS Region via Service Link

content.text: Three scenarios where public cloud regions are not sufficient drive Outposts adoption:

— a manufacturing line, trading exchange, or MRI scanner cannot tolerate the 10–80 ms round-trip to the nearest region. Outposts puts the compute in the same building as the equipment, cutting RTT to sub-millisecond.
— some jurisdictions or contracts prohibit data from leaving a specific facility. An Outpost subnet is in the same VPC as your region but the data never crosses a WAN link at rest.
— Outpost workloads continue running when the Service Link to the parent region is temporarily down. Control-plane operations are degraded, but existing instances keep running.
An Outpost is not a mini-region. It has no Availability Zone redundancy of its own. For resilience, you combine an Outpost subnet with a region subnet and architect for failover between them.
AWS offers two physical product lines:
1–96 EC2 units, up to 96 TB EBS
Full 42U AWS rack (3 needed for large config)
Branch offices, retail stores, edge factories
(m5, c5, r5, g4dn, i3en). The specific instance types available depend on the Outpost configuration you order.

Every Outpost has a Local Gateway — the equivalent of an Internet Gateway but for your corporate network. The LGW lets EC2 instances on the Outpost subnet communicate directly with on-premises servers without traffic transiting the AWS region.
The LGW is attached to the VPC automatically when you create an Outpost subnet.
Add a route: destination = on-prem CIDR, target = local-gateway-id.
The LGW uses Route Table associations similar to subnet route tables.
Traffic between the Outpost instance and the AWS region travels over the Service Link (a Direct Connect or VPN connection you provision).

# Route traffic to on-premises 10.1.0.0/16 via LGW
aws ec2 create-route \
  --route-table-id rtb-0abc123 \
  --destination-cidr-block 10.1.0.0/16 \
  --local-gateway-id lgw-0def456
Setting up an Outpost is a multi-week procurement and installation process:
— go to AWS Outposts in the console, choose rack or server, select instance/storage config, provide delivery details.
— AWS provides a Site Readiness Checklist covering power (208–240 V, 30 A circuits per rack), cooling (≥5 kW per rack), physical space, and network connectivity (Direct Connect recommended).
— AWS ships the rack and an AWS engineer performs the physical install and cabling. Customer responsibility ends at the power and network ports.
— in the console, activate the Outpost, choose its parent region and Availability Zone (for IAM/control plane affiliation).
— create an Outpost subnet inside your VPC, specifying the Outpost ARN.

# Create a subnet on an Outpost
aws ec2 create-subnet \
  --vpc-id vpc-0abc \
  --cidr-block 10.0.10.0/24 \
  --outpost-arn arn:aws:outposts:us-east-1:123456789012:outpost/op-0abc123def456 \
  --availability-zone us-east-1a
Once the subnet exists, launching instances on the Outpost is identical to any EC2 launch — just specify the Outpost subnet:

aws ec2 run-instances \
  --image-id ami-0abcdef1234567890 \
  --instance-type m5.xlarge \
  --subnet-id subnet-0outpost123 \
  --security-group-ids sg-0xyz \
  --key-name my-keypair \
  --count 1

EBS volumes created in an Outpost subnet are physically stored on the Outpost hardware. Snapshots are sent to the parent region S3 bucket — this cross-region traffic is the one exception where data leaves the premises.
m5, c5, r5, g4dn, i3en families
S3 on Outposts is a distinct product that creates an S3 bucket physically stored on the Outpost. Unlike regular S3 — which stores data in a region — S3 on Outposts keeps objects on-premises:
Object capacity is provisioned (e.g., 48 TB, 96 TB, 192 TB, 384 TB configurations).
Access via S3 API using a special outpost ARN-based endpoint.
Replication to a region S3 bucket is optional but not default.
local applications that need S3 API semantics without transferring large datasets to the cloud.

# List S3 on Outposts buckets
aws s3control list-regional-buckets \
  --account-id 123456789012 \
  --outpost-id op-0abc123def456

# Access via outpost endpoint
aws s3api get-object \
  --bucket arn:aws:s3-outposts:us-east-1:123456789012:outpost/op-0abc/bucket/my-bucket \
  --key data/file.csv \
  output.csv
Infrastructure-as-code for Outposts follows the same AWS provider, with Outpost-specific attributes:
Outpost vs Local Zone vs Wavelength Zone
Because a single Outpost has no internal redundancy, the standard HA pattern spans the Outpost subnet with a region subnet:
ALB in both subnets, auto-scaling in both, read replicas in both. Route 53 health checks detect Outpost failure.
Outpost as primary (low latency), region as standby. RDS Multi-AZ replication. Failover via Route 53 failover routing policy.
EKS control plane in region. Node groups in both Outpost subnet and region subnet. Pod affinity rules keep latency-sensitive pods on Outpost.
AWS manages the hardware; you manage the workloads. Key operational tools:
same SSM agent, patch management, and Session Manager access on Outpost instances.
metrics for Outpost EC2, EBS, and services. Outpost capacity metric:
monitor the Direct Connect circuit or VPN. Loss of Service Link degrades IAM calls; existing instances stay running.

# Check Outpost capacity utilization
aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
  --namespace AWS/Outposts \
  --metric-name InstanceTypeCapacityUtilization \
  --dimensions Name=OutpostId,Value=op-0abc123def456 \
               Name=InstanceType,Value=m5.xlarge \
  --start-time 2024-01-01T00:00:00Z \
  --end-time   2024-01-02T00:00:00Z \
  --period 3600 --statistics Average
an Outpost rack requires ~5–15 kW of power and appropriate airflow. Hardware failure recovery is AWS's responsibility but requires on-site access for physical swaps.
if the Direct Connect or VPN link goes down, you cannot launch new instances, create volumes, or make IAM calls. Plan workloads to be resilient to temporary control-plane outage.
snapshot data crosses the WAN. If data residency is strict, evaluate snapshot encryption and disable automatic snapshots until policy is clarified.
not all EC2 instance types are available. Confirm supported types before committing to a capacity order.
Outpost procurement and delivery typically takes 8–12 weeks. Plan ahead for new facilities.
Elastic IPs and Internet Gateway traffic route through the parent region. True internet egress from Outpost requires hairpinning through the region.
Flat capacity fee regardless of usage
AWS replaces failed hardware at no extra cost
There is no upfront fee for the Outpost hardware itself — you pay for the EC2 and storage capacity you provision, similar to reserved instances.
EKS on Outposts: Local Cluster Mode

EKS offers two deployment modes for Outposts. The default mode keeps the Kubernetes control plane in the parent region. A newer option — EKS Local Cluster — runs the Kubernetes API server itself on the Outpost, surviving Service Link outages at the cost of additional Outpost compute.
Use EKS Local Cluster when continuity of Kubernetes scheduling during WAN outages is a requirement — for example, autonomous factory robots that must self-schedule pod restarts.

# Create EKS local cluster on Outpost
aws eks create-cluster \
  --name factory-cluster \
  --role-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/EKSRole \
  --resources-vpc-config \
      subnetIds=subnet-0outpost123,securityGroupIds=sg-0xyz \
  --outpost-config \
      outpostArns=arn:aws:outposts:us-east-1:123456789012:outpost/op-0abc,\
      controlPlaneInstanceType=m5.xlarge

RDS on Outposts supports MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQL Server database instances running entirely on Outpost hardware. The RDS managed service model applies — automated backups, minor version patching, and Multi-AZ configuration — but with one key constraint:
RDS Multi-AZ for Outposts requires a second Outpost or a region subnet as the standby. A single Outpost alone cannot provide RDS Multi-AZ redundancy.
stored as EBS snapshots, which are uploaded to region S3. Retention period is configurable (1–35 days).
can be created in the parent region, allowing read offload for regional consumers while writes stay on-premises.
available, with metrics forwarded to the region for storage and visualization.

# Create RDS instance on Outpost
aws rds create-db-instance \
  --db-instance-identifier factory-mysql \
  --db-instance-class db.m5.xlarge \
  --engine mysql \
  --master-username admin \
  --master-user-password '<password>' \
  --db-subnet-group-name outpost-db-subnet-group \
  # Subnet group must reference the Outpost subnet
  --multi-az  # requires region or second Outpost subnet
Computer vision defect detection on production line
Camera frames processed locally; <2 ms inference latency required
Algorithmic trading co-located with exchange matching engine
Sub-millisecond order routing; data must stay on-premises
HIPAA data residency; DICOM images cannot leave the hospital
Operates during WAN outage; needs local persistence
Seismic data processing at remote drill site
Petabytes of sensor data; impractical to transfer to cloud
Cannot use shared public cloud infrastructure
Outposts inherits the full AWS security model with additional physical security responsibilities:
AWS hardware has tamper-evident seals. Customers must restrict physical access to the rack. AWS SLAs require customer notification before any on-site AWS visit for maintenance.
EBS volumes use the same KMS-based encryption as region. CMKs stored in the region KMS endpoint (requires Service Link for decryption on startup).
Service Link traffic is encrypted. Application traffic on the LGW is unencrypted at the AWS layer — use TLS at the application layer.
function identically to region. NACLs also apply. Use them to segment Outpost instances from on-premises servers that shouldn't have access.
all API calls (EC2, RDS, EKS) logged to the parent region CloudTrail. Local traffic is not visible in CloudTrail.
The Service Link carries three types of traffic between the Outpost and its parent region:
EC2 metadata, IAM token validation, Systems Manager heartbeats — typically < 1 Mbps.
can saturate the link during heavy snapshot periods. Schedule snapshots during off-peak hours.
EKS/ECS container images pulled from the region ECR can be 500 MB–5 GB. Use ECR image caching or mirror images locally.
Pre-pull and cache on Outpost EBS

AWS recommends a dedicated Direct Connect connection for Outpost Service Link, with a minimum 1 Gbps port. VPN is supported but introduces variable latency that can affect control-plane responsiveness.
The most common operational problems with Outposts and how to diagnose them:
Service Link down or IAM unreachable
ping region endpoint; check DX/VPN status in console
Nitro hypervisor issue or misconfigured security group
Check SG allows TCP 80 from instance CIDR; try IMDSv2
Service Link bandwidth saturated during backup window
Shift backup window; enable incremental snapshots
EKS nodes NotReady after Service Link restore
Re-register nodes with new bootstrap token

For hardware-level issues (disk failure, NIC error), open an AWS Support case — AWS dispatches a field engineer to replace the failed component. Typical SLA is next-business-day for standard support, 4-hour for Enterprise.
Outpost capacity is pre-committed at order time. Unlike the public cloud where capacity is elastic, you must estimate your peak demand upfront:
an Outpost rack configured for m5.xlarge holds a fixed number of instance slots. Determine peak instance count from CloudWatch metrics before ordering.
EBS is shared across all instances. Monitor
and leave ≥ 20% headroom for snapshots and temporary volumes.
order 20–30% more capacity than current peak. Adding capacity later requires ordering additional Outpost hardware (another rack or upgrading server config).
a single Outpost can host multiple instance types if ordered with the appropriate capacity config. Specify this at order time via the Outpost configuration tool.

# List available capacity on your Outpost
aws outposts get-outpost-instance-types \
  --outpost-id op-0abc123def456

# Example output:
# { "InstanceTypes": [
#     { "InstanceType": "m5.xlarge", "VCPUs": "4", "MemoryInMiB": "16384" },
#     { "InstanceType": "c5.2xlarge", "VCPUs": "8", "MemoryInMiB": "16384" },
#     { "InstanceType": "r5.xlarge", "VCPUs": "4", "MemoryInMiB": "32768" }
# ] }
AWS Outposts extends a VPC subnet to your own data center, giving you AWS APIs on-premises with sub-millisecond latency to co-located systems.
AWS owns and manages the hardware; you own the space, power, and network connectivity.
The Local Gateway (LGW) enables direct communication between Outpost instances and on-premises servers without transiting the AWS region.
Service Link (Direct Connect / VPN) connects the Outpost to the parent region for control-plane operations. Existing workloads continue during Service Link outages.
Choose Outposts for full data residency control, ultra-low latency to on-premises equipment, or regulatory requirements. Choose Local Zones for metro-scale latency without hardware ownership.
